# Manifest — Full Documentation

> This file contains the complete text of all Manifest documentation pages.

> Source: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app

> Package: @angriff36/manifest v2.18.6

<!-- NOTE: This file is a point-in-time snapshot of the documentation body and is not auto-regenerated. The version banner above has been updated to the current release, but the prose below may reflect an earlier docs state. Treat the live docs pages as authoritative. -->


---

## Introduction

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/introduction


Manifest is a formal language for expressing domain logic — the rules, constraints, and operations that define how your application's data actually behaves. You write `.manifest` source files that describe entities with typed properties, commands that mutate state, guards that gate execution, policies that enforce authorization, and events that notify downstream systems. The compiler transforms that source into a compact Intermediate Representation (IR), and the `RuntimeEngine` executes the IR deterministically — enforcing every guard and policy in strict order, every time.

## The core mental model

Every Manifest program moves through two stages:

1. **Compile** — your `.manifest` source is parsed and transformed to IR v1 JSON. The IR is the canonical contract between the compiler and everything downstream.
2. **Execute** — the `RuntimeEngine` accepts the IR plus a runtime context (user identity, tenant, etc.) and runs commands. Execution always follows the same order: policies → guards → actions → event emissions.

Because the IR is immutable at runtime, all variability enters through the runtime context. Identical IR plus identical context always produces identical results.

## Key language constructs

| Construct | What it does |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Entity** | Defines a data structure with typed properties, defaults, computed values, and relationships |
| **Command** | A named business operation with parameters, guards, actions (`mutate`, `emit`, `compute`), and event emissions |
| **Guard** | A boolean expression evaluated in order; execution halts immediately on the first falsey guard |
| **Policy** | An authorization rule scoped to an entity action (`read`, `write`, `execute`, `all`) |
| **Event** | A typed definition with a channel name and payload schema, used for outbox patterns and real-time communication |
| **Store** | Declares where entity instances persist — `memory` for development, or a database adapter for production |

## A real example

The following entity models a kitchen prep task. It defines computed properties, guards that restrict who can run each command, and events emitted on state transitions.

```manifest
entity PrepTask {
  property required id: string
  property required name: string
  property assignedTo: string?
  property status: string = "pending"
  property priority: number = 1

  computed isUrgent: boolean = priority >= 3

  command claim(employeeId: string) {
    guard self.status == "pending"
    guard user.role == "cook" or user.role == "chef"
    mutate assignedTo = employeeId
    mutate status = "in_progress"
    emit taskClaimed
  }

  command complete() {
    guard self.status == "in_progress"
    guard self.assignedTo == user.id
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit taskCompleted
  }

  policy canClaim execute: user.role in ["cook", "chef"]
}

store PrepTask in memory

event taskClaimed: "kitchen.task.claimed" {
  taskId: string
  employeeId: string
}

event taskCompleted: "kitchen.task.completed" {
  taskId: string
  completedBy: string
}
```

## How Manifest differs from ORMs

ORMs map database tables to objects and handle queries. Manifest models domain _behavior_ — the rules and operations that govern what can happen to your data, not just its shape. Where an ORM lets you call `update()` freely, Manifest requires that every state change pass through a command with explicit guards and policy checks. The runtime refuses to skip them.

This means your business rules live in one place — the `.manifest` file — rather than scattered across route handlers, service classes, and middleware.

## Two integration patterns

Most applications use one or both of:

- **Projections** — run `manifest build` to auto-generate Next.js API routes directly from your `.manifest` files. Best for standard CRUD workflows.
- **Embedded runtime** — import `RuntimeEngine` and call `runCommand()` directly in your own handlers. Best for complex workflows or when you need full control.

## Where to go next

<CardGroup cols={3}>
  <Card title="Quickstart" icon="bolt" href="/quickstart">
    Install the package and run your first command in under five minutes.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Installation" icon="package" href="/installation">
    Full install options, package exports, TypeScript setup, and CLI reference.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Language reference" icon="book" href="/language/entities">
    Complete specification for entities, commands, guards, policies, events, and stores.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>


---

## Quickstart

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/quickstart


This guide walks you from a blank project to a running Manifest program. You will install the package, write a `.manifest` source file, compile it to IR, and call `RuntimeEngine.runCommand()` to see the result. All code examples use real APIs from the package — nothing is invented.

<Steps>

<Step title="Install the package">

Add `@angriff36/manifest` to your project. Node.js 18 or later is required.



```bash npm
npm install @angriff36/manifest
```

```bash pnpm
pnpm add @angriff36/manifest
```



</Step>

<Step title="Write your first .manifest file">

Create `my-program.manifest` in your project root. This entity models a simple to-do item with two commands and an authorization policy.

```manifest
entity Todo {
  property id: string
  property title: string
  property completed: boolean
  property createdAt: timestamp

  command create(title: string) {
    guard title is not empty
    guard title.length < 100

    mutate this.title = title
    mutate this.completed = false
    mutate this.createdAt = now()
  }

  command complete() {
    guard this.completed is false

    mutate this.completed = true
    emit TodoCompleted { todoId: this.id }
  }

  policy read allow if user.role == "admin"
  policy execute allow if user.id == this.createdBy
}
```

Each `command` defines a named business operation. The `guard` lines gate execution — if any guard evaluates to `false`, the command stops immediately and returns diagnostics. The `policy` lines enforce authorization before guards even run.

</Step>

<Step title="Compile the source to IR">

Use the `compileToIR` export to parse the source and produce an IR object. Always check diagnostics before proceeding — compilation errors are surfaced here, not thrown as exceptions.

```typescript
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
import { readFileSync } from 'fs';

const source = readFileSync('my-program.manifest', 'utf-8');

const { ir, diagnostics } = await compileToIR(source);

if (diagnostics.some(d => d.severity === 'error')) {
  console.error('Compilation errors:');
  diagnostics.forEach(d => console.error(`  ${d.message}`));
  process.exit(1);
}

console.log('Compiled IR:', JSON.stringify(ir, null, 2));
```

You can also compile inline source directly — useful for tests and prototyping:

```typescript
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';

const source = `
  entity Todo {
    property id: string
    property title: string
  }
`;

const { ir, diagnostics } = await compileToIR(source);
```


  The IR is plain JSON. You can serialize it to disk, cache it, or pass it to any downstream consumer — including the `RuntimeEngine`, projections, and conformance tooling.


</Step>

<Step title="Execute a command with RuntimeEngine">

Import `RuntimeEngine` from the package root and construct it with your compiled IR and a runtime context. The context provides the user identity and tenant that guards and policies resolve against.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  userId: 'user-123',
  tenantId: 'tenant-456',
});

// Run the "create" command on the Todo entity
const createResult = await runtime.runCommand('Todo', 'create', {
  title: 'Learn Manifest'
});

if (!createResult.success) {
  console.error('Failed:', createResult.diagnostics);
  process.exit(1);
}

console.log('Created:', createResult.instance);
```

When the command succeeds, `createResult.instance` contains the resulting entity state. When it fails — because a guard evaluated to `false` or a policy denied access — `createResult.diagnostics` tells you exactly which check failed and what values were resolved.

</Step>

<Step title="Chain a second command and inspect events">

Use the instance returned by `create` to run `complete`. Check `completeResult.events` to see the `TodoCompleted` event that the command emitted.

```typescript
const completeResult = await runtime.runCommand('Todo', 'complete', {
  id: createResult.instance.id
});

if (completeResult.success) {
  console.log('Events:', completeResult.events);
  // Output: Events: [{ name: 'TodoCompleted', channel: 'default', ... }]
}
```

You can also subscribe to events using `runtime.on`:

```typescript
runtime.on('TodoCompleted', (event) => {
  console.log('Todo completed:', event.payload.todoId);

  // Trigger side effects
  sendEmail(event.payload.todoId);
  updateAnalytics(event.payload.todoId);
});
```

</Step>

</Steps>

## Running the CLI instead

If you prefer a command-line workflow, use the `manifest` CLI that ships with the package:

```bash
# Compile a .manifest file to IR
manifest compile my-program.manifest --output ir/

# Compile and generate Next.js routes in one step
manifest build my-program.manifest --projection nextjs --output app/api/

# Interactive project setup (creates manifest.config.yaml)
manifest init
```


  Run `manifest init` once to create a `manifest.config.yaml` for your project. After that, `manifest build` uses your config defaults and you don't need to repeat flags on every invocation.


## Troubleshooting

**"Entity not found in IR"** — Entity names are case-sensitive. Make sure the name you pass to `runCommand` exactly matches the `entity` declaration in your source file.

**"Guard failed but no diagnostic"** — Check that you aren't silently catching errors before inspecting `result.diagnostics`. The runtime always emits a diagnostic for every guard failure, including the guard index, the expression, and resolved sub-expression values.

**Compilation errors after a small change** — Conformance tests treat compilation output as executable semantics. If something stops compiling, you've changed language meaning. Review your source against the language specification.

## Next steps

- **[Installation](/installation)** — full install options, all package exports, TypeScript configuration, and CLI setup
- **[Language reference](/language/entities)** — complete specification for entities, commands, guards, policies, events, and stores
- **[Integration patterns](/integration/overview)** — how to use projections and the embedded runtime together in a real application


---

## Installation

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/installation


Manifest is published as `@angriff36/manifest` to GitHub Packages. Before installing, make sure your project meets the minimum requirements: **Node.js 18 or later** and a package manager that supports scoped registries (npm or pnpm).


  **Current version:** `2.10.2`. (Historical: v1.0.0 introduced composite PK/FK, referential actions, and the Prisma projection.) This snapshot's prose may lag the live docs; the live pages are authoritative.


## Install the package



```bash npm
npm install @angriff36/manifest
```

```bash pnpm
pnpm add @angriff36/manifest
```




  `@angriff36/manifest` is published to GitHub Packages, not the public npm registry. You need a GitHub personal access token with `read:packages` scope and an `.npmrc` entry that routes `@angriff36` to `https://npm.pkg.github.com`. See GitHub's [Authenticating to GitHub Packages](https://docs.github.com/en/packages/working-with-a-github-packages-registry/working-with-the-npm-registry#authenticating-to-github-packages) documentation for setup instructions.


## Package exports

The package ships multiple named entry points. Import only what you need — each export is independent.

| Import path | What it provides |
|-------------|-----------------|
| `@angriff36/manifest` | `RuntimeEngine` — the main command execution engine |
| `@angriff36/manifest/compiler` | The high-level `compiler` orchestrator |
| `@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler` | `compileToIR` — parse source and produce IR v1 |
| `@angriff36/manifest/ir` | IR data structure types |
| `@angriff36/manifest/stores` | Node.js store adapters |
| `@angriff36/manifest/projections/nextjs` | Next.js code generation projection |
| `@angriff36/manifest/projections/routes` | Route generation projection |
| `@angriff36/manifest/registry/emit` | Event registry emit helpers |
| `@angriff36/manifest/audit` | `AuditSink` interface |
| `@angriff36/manifest/audit/memory` | `MemoryAuditSink` for development and tests |
| `@angriff36/manifest/audit/postgres` | `PostgresAuditSink` for production |
| `@angriff36/manifest/outbox` | `OutboxStore` interface |
| `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/memory` | `MemoryOutboxStore` for development and tests |
| `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres` | `PostgresOutboxStore` for production |

The most common imports for getting started are:

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
```

## TypeScript setup

The package ships declaration files for every export. No `@types/` package is needed. Your `tsconfig.json` must target **ES2020 or later** and enable `moduleResolution: "bundler"` or `"node16"`/`"nodenext"` to resolve the package's `exports` map correctly.

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "strict": true
  }
}
```


  The package is published as ESM (`"type": "module"`). If your project uses CommonJS, configure your bundler or TypeScript to handle ESM interop, or use a bundler like Vite or esbuild that handles this automatically.


## Optional peer dependency: Supabase

The Supabase adapter is an optional peer dependency. Install it only if you plan to use Supabase as a backing store:

```bash
npm install @supabase/supabase-js
```

The package declares `@supabase/supabase-js@^2` as an optional peer. If it is not installed, all non-Supabase store targets continue to work normally.

## Install the CLI

The `manifest` CLI ships inside the package under `packages/cli/dist/index.js` and is registered as the `manifest` binary. It is available immediately after installing `@angriff36/manifest`:

```bash
# Verify the CLI is available
npx manifest --help
```

The CLI provides four main commands:

```bash
# Interactive project setup — creates manifest.config.yaml
manifest init

# Compile .manifest source to IR
manifest compile modules/recipe.manifest --output ir/

# Generate code from IR using a projection
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --projection nextjs --output app/api/

# Compile + generate in one step (recommended after manifest init)
manifest build modules/recipe.manifest
```

If you prefer to install the CLI as a dev dependency explicitly, it is also published separately:

```bash
npm install -D @manifest/cli
```


  Run `manifest init` once at the start of a project. It creates `manifest.config.yaml` with your framework, auth provider, import paths, and output directory. After that, `manifest build` reads those defaults and you only need to pass flags when overriding them.


## Verifying your install

Run the following to confirm the package resolves correctly and the CLI responds:

```bash
# Check CLI
npx manifest --help

# Smoke-test the compiler from a script
node --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
const { ir, diagnostics } = await compileToIR('entity Ping { property id: string }');
console.log('IR version:', ir?.version ?? 'compiled OK');
EOF
```

## Windows note

On Windows with pnpm, the `manifest` shim may exit with code `0` and no output on older versions. If this happens, invoke the CLI directly:

```bash
pnpm exec manifest --help
# or
node .\node_modules\@manifest\runtime\packages\cli\dist\index.js --help
```

This was fixed in `0.3.10` — updating to the latest version resolves it.


---

## Entities

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/entities


An entity is the primary building block in Manifest. It combines a typed data schema with behavioral constraints, commands, and computed properties into a single declaration. The compiler turns entities into IR that the runtime can instantiate, query, and mutate.

## Basic declaration

Use the `entity` keyword followed by a name and a block. Property declarations go inside the block.

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required name: string
  property price: number = 0
  property inStock: boolean = true
  property description: string = ""
}
```

## External entities

Prefix the `entity` keyword with `external` to declare that an entity is **referenced but not persisted by this manifest**:

```manifest
external entity ImportedUser {
  property required id: string
  property required email: string
}
```

`external` is a binary marker. Default (absent) means owned. Storage projections (e.g. the [Prisma projection](/integration/prisma)) **skip external entities entirely** — no model is emitted, no table is created. Other projections may use the flag as they see fit, or ignore it.

The IR carries this as a sparse-optional field: `IREntity.external?: boolean`. When absent the entity is owned (the default); when present and `true` the entity is external. Legacy IR fixtures stay byte-identical because the field is only emitted when set.


`external` means exactly "skip this entity in storage projections". It does not affect runtime semantics, command execution, or any other downstream tool. The marker is intentionally narrow — if a future case needs "external but generate types", that will be a separate flag, not a redefinition of this one.


## Properties

Every property has a type. Supported types are `string`, `number`, `boolean`, and `timestamp`. A property without a default is `undefined` unless the caller supplies a value at creation time.

### Modifiers

Place modifiers between the `property` keyword and the name:

| Modifier   | Meaning |
|------------|---------|
| `required` | The caller must supply a value (or a `defaultValue` must exist). |
| `optional` | Explicitly marks the property as not required. Equivalent to the default behavior. |
| `readonly` | Prevents mutation after creation (enforced by convention; scanner warns on violation). |
| `unique`   | Signals to adapters that values must be unique within the entity collection. |

```manifest
entity User {
  property required id: string
  property required email: string
  property optional displayName: string = ""
  property readonly createdAt: number = 0
}
```

### Default values

Any property can carry a literal default. When you create an instance and omit that property, the runtime applies the default. Explicitly supplying a value — including an empty string `""` — skips the default entirely.

```manifest
entity Task {
  property status: string = "pending"
  property priority: number = 1
  property completed: boolean = false
}
```


Defaults apply only when the property is **omitted** from the creation payload. An empty string `""` counts as a provided value and overrides the default.


## Relationships

Relationships declare connections between entities. The runtime resolves them synchronously from the current store context.

| Keyword     | Cardinality | Returns |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| `hasMany`   | One-to-many | Array of related instances (never `null`; empty array when none) |
| `hasOne`    | One-to-one  | Single related instance or `null` |
| `belongsTo` | Many-to-one | Single related instance or `null` |
| `ref`       | Simple reference | Single related instance or `null` |

```manifest
entity Author {
  property required name: string
  hasMany books: Book
  computed bookCount: number = self.books.length
}

entity Book {
  property required title: string
  property year: number = 2024
  belongsTo author: Author
}
```

For `belongsTo` and `ref`, the foreign key property on the source instance holds the target ID. For `hasMany` and `hasOne`, the runtime queries the target entity for instances whose foreign key matches the current instance's ID.


Accessing a relationship on a non-existent instance returns `null` for singular relationships and `[]` for `hasMany`. Circular relationships are handled gracefully.


## Constraints

Constraints are boolean expressions that validate entity state. You can attach them at the entity level (evaluated whenever an instance is created or mutated) or at the command level (evaluated before that command runs — see [Commands](/language/commands)).

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required name: string
  property price: number = 0

  constraint pricePositive: self.price > 0 "Price must be positive"
  constraint nameNotEmpty: self.name != "" "Name cannot be empty"
}
```

### Constraint severity

Each constraint has a severity that controls what happens when it evaluates to `false`:

| Severity | Behavior |
|----------|----------|
| `ok`     | Informational only. The outcome is always `passed` regardless of the expression result. |
| `warn`   | Produces a `ConstraintOutcome` with the real result but does **not** halt execution. |
| `block`  | Halts execution when the expression evaluates to `false` (default). |

```manifest
entity Task {
  property status: string = "pending"
  property priority: number = 1
  property dueDate: number = 0

  constraint okInfo {
    expression: priority > 0
    severity: ok
    message: "Priority is positive"
  }

  constraint warnHighPriority {
    expression: priority <= 5
    severity: warn
    message: "Priority is higher than recommended"
  }

  constraint blockStatus {
    expression: status in ["pending", "in_progress", "completed", "cancelled"]
    severity: block
    message: "Status must be one of the allowed values"
  }
}
```

The long-form block syntax also supports `messageTemplate` and `details` for structured diagnostics:

```manifest
constraint amountLimit:block self.amount > 10000 {
  messageTemplate: "Amount {currentAmount} exceeds limit of {maxAmount}"
  details: {
    maxAmount: 10000
    currentAmount: self.amount
    excessAmount: self.amount - 10000
  }
}
```

### Overrideable constraints

Mark a `block` constraint `overrideable` to allow authorized bypass at runtime. Pair it with an `overridePolicy` to control who can authorize the override.

```manifest
entity FinancialRecord {
  property required amount: number = 0
  property required status: string = "pending"

  command process() {
    constraint overrideable requiresApproval {
      expression: self.status == "approved" or self.amount <= 500
      severity: block
      message: "Must be approved before processing"
      overridePolicy: canApproveLargeAmounts
    }

    mutate status = "processed"
    emit RecordProcessed
  }
}

policy canApproveLargeAmounts override:
  user.role == "manager" or user.role == "admin"
  "Only managers and admins can approve large amounts"
```

When a caller supplies an `OverrideRequest` matching the constraint code, the runtime checks the policy, marks the outcome `overridden: true`, and emits a synthetic `OverrideApplied` event.

## Composite keys and foreign key structure

### Composite primary keys

Declare `key [field1, field2]` inside an entity to define a composite primary key. The listed fields together form the entity's identity — no single `id` field is needed.

```manifest
entity Order {
  key [tenantId, id]
  property required tenantId: string
  property required id: string
  property total: number = 0
}
```

When the Prisma projection sees a `key`, it emits `@@id([tenantId, id])` instead of a single `@id` field. The runtime still uses the named fields but treats the entity as identified by the tuple.

### Alternate keys

Declare `unique [field1, field2]` to define a non-PK uniqueness constraint (an alternate key). You can declare multiple.

```manifest
entity AdminChatParticipant {
  key [tenantId, id]
  unique [tenantId, threadId, userId]
  property required tenantId: string
  property required id: string
  property required threadId: string
  property required userId: string
}
```

The Prisma projection emits `@@unique([...])` for each group. Other projections use it to derive uniqueness constraints on the target platform.

### Composite foreign keys

For `belongsTo` and `ref` relationships, declare the FK columns explicitly when your relationship spans multiple columns:

```manifest
// Single-column FK using the shorthand (backward-compatible)
belongsTo author: Author with authorId

// Composite FK — explicit fields and references
belongsTo thread: AdminChatThread fields [tenantId, threadId] references [tenantId, id] onDelete cascade

// Cross-order composite FK
belongsTo proposal: Proposal fields [proposalId, tenantId] references [id, tenantId] onDelete cascade
```

`fields` names properties on the current entity; `references` names the corresponding columns on the target. If `references` is omitted, `[id]` is assumed.

**Runtime note**: The Manifest runtime uses a single-key in-memory store. Composite FKs are a projection concern — the runtime only resolves single-column FKs. A `belongsTo` with `fields.length > 1` returns `null` at runtime. Use a `storeProvider` adapter backed by a real database when composite-FK resolution is required.

### Referential actions

Add `onDelete` and/or `onUpdate` to any `belongsTo` or `ref` to declare referential integrity behavior. Valid values: `cascade`, `restrict`, `setNull`, `setDefault`, `noAction`.

```manifest
belongsTo tenant: Account with tenantId onDelete restrict
belongsTo item: LineItem fields [tenantId, itemId] references [tenantId, id] onDelete cascade onUpdate restrict
```

When no action is declared, the projection emits nothing and the target platform uses its own default.

## Concurrency controls (vNext)

Entities can declare optimistic concurrency controls using `versionProperty` and `versionAtProperty`. The runtime compares the caller-supplied version against the stored value on every mutation.

```manifest
entity Document {
  property required id: string = ""
  property title: string = ""
  property content: string = ""

  versionProperty version: number
  versionAtProperty versionAt: number

  command update(newTitle: string, currentVersion: number) {
    guard self.version == currentVersion
    mutate title = newTitle
    emit DocumentUpdated
  }
}
```

If the caller's version does not match, the runtime returns a `ConcurrencyConflict` and does not apply any mutations:

```typescript
{
  entityType: "Document",
  entityId: "doc-1",
  expectedVersion: 2,
  actualVersion: 3,
  conflictCode: "VERSION_MISMATCH"
}
```

## State transitions (vNext)

Declare allowed state changes with the `transition` keyword. The runtime validates every mutation of the property against the rules before applying it.

```manifest
entity Document {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "draft"

  transition status from "draft"     to ["review", "archived"]
  transition status from "review"    to ["published", "draft"]
  transition status from "published" to ["archived"]

  command submit() {
    mutate status = "review"
  }

  command approve() {
    mutate status = "published"
  }

  command archive() {
    mutate status = "archived"
  }
}
```


Transition validation runs **before** entity constraint validation. If the current value has no matching `from` rule, the transition is unconstrained from that state. Properties not mentioned in any transition rule are always unconstrained.


## Default policies (vNext)

Attach a `default policy` to an entity to apply it to every command that does not declare its own policy list:

```manifest
entity Task {
  property required title: string
  property status: string = "pending"

  default policy RequireAuth execute: user.id != null "Authentication required"

  command complete() {
    guard self.assigneeId == user.id or user.role == "admin"
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit TaskCompleted
  }

  command reassign(newAssigneeId: string) {
    guard user.role == "admin" or user.role == "manager"
    mutate assigneeId = newAssigneeId
  }
}
```

Both `complete` and `reassign` inherit `RequireAuth` because neither declares an explicit policy. See [Guards and Policies](/language/guards-policies) for the full inheritance rules.


---

## Commands

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/commands


A command is a named operation bound to an entity. When you call `runCommand`, the runtime executes the command in a strict, deterministic order: policies → constraints → guards → actions → emits → return. Nothing in that order can be rearranged or skipped.

## Declaring a command

Commands live inside an entity block. A command takes an optional parameter list and a body of guards and actions.

```manifest
entity Counter {
  property value: number = 0

  command increment() {
    mutate value = value + 1
    emit CounterIncremented
  }

  command setValue(newValue: number) {
    guard newValue >= 0
    mutate value = newValue
    emit CounterSet
  }
}
```

Parameters are typed with the same types as properties: `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `timestamp`. Parameters are available by name in all guard and action expressions.

## Execution order

The runtime executes every command in exactly this order:

<Steps>
  <Step title="Build evaluation context">
    Bind `self` and `this` to the current entity instance, inject command parameters, and attach the runtime context (`user`, `context.*`).
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate policies">
    Check every applicable policy (entity-level defaults first, then command-level). If any policy denies, execution stops immediately with a `policyDenial` result.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate command constraints">
    Evaluate any `block` constraints declared inside the command. If any fail without an authorized override, execution stops with `constraintOutcomes` in the result.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate guards">
    Evaluate each guard expression in declaration order. Execution halts on the first falsey result and returns a `guardFailure` with the failing guard's index and expression.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Execute actions">
    Run each action (`mutate`, `emit`, `compute`, `publish`, `persist`, `effect`) in declaration order.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Emit declared events">
    Emit the events listed in the `emits` declaration (if any) in order, attaching payload and metadata.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Return CommandResult">
    Return the `CommandResult` with `success: true`, the last action result, and all emitted events.
  </Step>
</Steps>

## Actions

Actions are the statements inside a command body that produce effects.

### `mutate`

Assigns a value to an entity property. The expression is evaluated against the current context and stored on the instance.

```manifest
command complete() {
  guard not self.completed
  mutate completed = true
  mutate completedAt = now()
}
```

### `emit`

Emits an event and optionally attaches an inline payload. Also produces an action result with the evaluated value.

```manifest
command increment() {
  mutate value = value + 1
  emit CounterIncremented
}
```

### `compute`

Evaluates an expression and returns the value in the action result.

**Store-write behavior:** `compute` writes to the store when **all three** are true: the action has a `target` property name, the command was called with an `instanceId`, and it has an `entityName`. When any of those is absent, `compute` is pure — it returns the value without persisting. Use `effect` if you need a derivation that must never write to the store.

```manifest
command setTimestamp() {
  compute createdAt = now()
  emit TaskCreated
}
```

### `publish`

Sends the expression result to an external message broker or outbox adapter. In the default (no-adapter) runtime, behaves like `compute`. In deterministic mode, throws `ManifestEffectBoundaryError`.

### `persist`

Invokes a persistence adapter with the expression result. In the default runtime, behaves like `compute`. In deterministic mode, throws `ManifestEffectBoundaryError`.

### `effect`

Invokes a side-effect adapter. In the default runtime, behaves like `compute`. In deterministic mode, throws `ManifestEffectBoundaryError`.


`publish`, `persist`, and `effect` are no-ops unless you configure an adapter. When `deterministicMode` is `true`, calling any of them throws `ManifestEffectBoundaryError` — this is by design to enforce the effect boundary contract.


## The `emits` keyword

You can declare events at the command level using the `emits` keyword outside the action body. This serves as a declaration that the command emits a specific event after all actions succeed.

```manifest
entity Order {
  property status: string = "pending"

  command complete() {
    guard self.status == "in_progress"
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit OrderCompleted
  }
}

event OrderCompleted: "order.completed" {
  orderId: string
  completedAt: number
}
```

See [Events](/language/events) for how to declare event schemas.

## Command constraints (vNext)

Commands can declare their own `constraint` blocks that are evaluated after policies but before guards. The same severity levels (`ok`, `warn`, `block`) apply:

```manifest
entity Order {
  property status: string = "pending"
  property amount: number = 0

  command updateStatus(newStatus: string) {
    constraint notCancelled: self.status != "cancelled" "Cannot update a cancelled order"
    constraint amountVerified:warn self.amount >= 100 "Orders under $100 require manual approval"

    mutate status = newStatus
    emit OrderStatusChanged
  }
}
```

A failing `block` constraint stops execution and surfaces `constraintOutcomes` in the `CommandResult`. A `warn` constraint produces an outcome entry but does not stop execution.

## CommandResult shape

Every call to `runCommand` returns a `CommandResult`:

```typescript
interface CommandResult {
  success: boolean;
  error?: string;

  // Present when a policy denied execution
  policyDenial?: {
    policyName: string;
    expression: string;
  };

  // Present when a guard halted execution
  guardFailure?: {
    index: number;            // 1-based
    expression: string;
    resolved: { expression: string; value: unknown }[];
  };

  // Present when constraints were evaluated
  constraintOutcomes?: ConstraintOutcome[];

  // Present when optimistic concurrency detected a conflict
  concurrencyConflict?: ConcurrencyConflict;

  // All events emitted during this run
  emittedEvents: EmittedEvent[];

  // The return value of the last action
  lastActionResult?: unknown;
}
```

## Calling `runCommand` from TypeScript

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from "@angriff36/manifest";
import { ir } from "./compiled.ir.json";

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: "user-123", role: "admin" },
});

const result = await runtime.runCommand("Order", "updateStatus", {
  instanceId: "order-abc",
  newStatus: "processing",
});

if (!result.success) {
  if (result.policyDenial) {
    console.error("Denied by policy:", result.policyDenial.policyName);
  } else if (result.guardFailure) {
    console.error(
      `Guard ${result.guardFailure.index} failed:`,
      result.guardFailure.expression
    );
  } else if (result.constraintOutcomes) {
    const blocked = result.constraintOutcomes.filter(
      (o) => o.severity === "block" && !o.passed
    );
    console.error("Blocked by constraints:", blocked.map((o) => o.code));
  }
  process.exit(1);
}

console.log("Events:", result.emittedEvents);
```


The `instanceId` is passed inside the options object to `runCommand`, not as a command parameter. Command parameters are the named values declared in the command signature.


---

## Guards and Policies

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/guards-policies


Manifest separates authorization into two distinct mechanisms: **policies**, which control who is allowed to execute a command at all, and **guards**, which verify that the current state satisfies the preconditions for the command to proceed. Both are evaluated before any action runs, and neither can be bypassed.

## Guards

A guard is a boolean expression declared inside a command body. Guards are evaluated in declaration order. Execution halts on the first expression that evaluates to `false` — no later guards run, and no actions execute.

```manifest
entity Task {
  property completed: boolean = false
  property priority: number = 1

  command complete() {
    guard not self.completed
    mutate completed = true
    emit TaskCompleted
  }

  command setPriority(level: number) {
    guard level >= 1 and level <= 5
    mutate priority = level
  }
}
```

### Guard diagnostics

When a guard fails, the runtime returns a `guardFailure` in the `CommandResult` with:

- `index` — 1-based position of the failing guard
- `expression` — the source expression as a string
- `resolved` — array of `{ expression, value }` pairs showing what each sub-expression evaluated to

This gives you enough information to explain the failure to the caller without any manual debugging.

```typescript
if (result.guardFailure) {
  console.error(
    `Guard ${result.guardFailure.index} failed: ${result.guardFailure.expression}`
  );
  for (const r of result.guardFailure.resolved) {
    console.error(`  ${r.expression} => ${r.value}`);
  }
}
```

### Available bindings in guards

Guard expressions evaluate in the command's evaluation context. You have access to:

| Binding       | Value |
|---------------|-------|
| `self` / `this` | The current entity instance (or `null` when no instance is bound) |
| `user`        | The current user object, or `null` when unauthenticated |
| `context`     | The runtime context object |
| `context.tenantId` | Active tenant ID (if provided by caller) |
| `context.orgId` | Active organization ID (if provided) |
| `context.actorId` | Acting user ID (if provided) |
| `context.requestId` | Caller-supplied request ID |
| Command params | Named parameters from the command signature |
| `now()`       | Current time as milliseconds since epoch |
| `uuid()`      | A new globally unique identifier |


If a guard references `user` and no user is provided to the runtime, the guard correctly fails. This is not a bug — it is the expected behavior. The runtime does not inject a permissive default `user` to make demos work.


### Operators in guard expressions

Guards support the full expression language:

| Category | Operators |
|----------|-----------|
| Arithmetic | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` |
| Comparison | `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` |
| Logical | `and`, `or`, `not`, `!` |
| Membership | `in`, `contains` |
| Conditional | `? :` (ternary) |

`==` uses loose equality (`undefined == null` is `true`). Use `is not` or `!=` for strict inequality.

## Policies

A policy is a named boolean expression with a **scope** that controls when it is checked. Policies are declared outside entity blocks (or as entity-level defaults in vNext).

```manifest
policy adminOnly execute: user.role == "admin" "Only administrators can execute commands"
```

The expression after the scope keyword is evaluated before any command runs. If it returns `false`, the runtime returns a `policyDenial` result and no further execution occurs.

### Policy scopes

| Scope     | When enforced |
|-----------|---------------|
| `execute` | Before any command execution |
| `read`    | On entity read operations (not enforced by default runtime) |
| `write`   | On entity write operations (not enforced by default runtime) |
| `all`     | Equivalent to `execute` in the default runtime |
| `override` | Before authorizing a constraint override (vNext) |

The runtime enforces `execute` and `all` scopes by default. `read`, `write`, and `delete` scopes are not enforced by the default runtime — they are available for custom adapter or projection logic.

### Binding a policy to an entity

A policy applies to all commands on a specific entity when you reference it inside the entity block or use the entity name in a standalone policy:

```manifest
entity Document {
  property required title: string
  property isPublic: boolean = false

  command makePublic() {
    mutate isPublic = true
    emit DocumentPublished
  }
}

policy adminOnly execute: user.role == "admin" "Only admins can execute"
```

In this example, `adminOnly` is a global policy. Bind it to a specific command by listing it inside the command body, or use `default policy` to apply it to every command on the entity.

### Command-level policies

Declare a policy inline within a command to override the entity's defaults:

```manifest
entity InventoryItem {
  default policy execute: user.role in ["kitchen_staff", "kitchen_lead", "manager", "admin"]

  command consume(quantity: number) {
    // Inherits default policy
    guard quantity > 0
    mutate stock = self.stock - quantity
  }

  command adjust(delta: number) {
    // Overrides default — managers only
    policy execute: user.role in ["kitchen_lead", "manager", "admin"]
    mutate stock = self.stock + delta
  }
}
```


When a command declares its own `policy` list, the entity's `defaultPolicies` are **not merged** — the command's explicit policies fully replace the defaults.


## Default policies (vNext)

Use `default policy` on an entity to apply a policy to every command that does not declare its own:

```manifest
entity Task {
  property required title: string
  property status: string = "pending"
  property assigneeId: string

  default policy RequireAuth execute: user.id != null "Authentication required"

  command complete() {
    guard self.assigneeId == user.id or user.role == "admin"
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit TaskCompleted
  }

  command reassign(newAssigneeId: string) {
    guard user.role == "admin" or user.role == "manager"
    mutate assigneeId = newAssigneeId
  }
}
```

Both `complete` and `reassign` inherit `RequireAuth`. If you add a command with an explicit `policy` declaration, that command uses only its declared policy.

### Inheritance rules

1. If the command declares explicit policies, use only those.
2. If the command has no explicit policies but the entity has `defaultPolicies`, use the entity defaults.
3. If neither has policies, no policy check runs (the scanner will warn).

The IR compiler expands default policies into each command's effective policy list at compile time. The runtime always evaluates an explicit, expanded list — it does not perform inheritance at runtime.

## Override policies (vNext)

The `override` scope is a special policy used to authorize constraint bypasses. When a constraint is marked `overrideable`, the runtime evaluates the referenced override policy before granting the bypass:

```manifest
entity FinancialRecord {
  command process() {
    constraint overrideable requiresApproval {
      expression: self.status == "approved" or self.amount <= 500
      severity: block
      message: "Must be approved before processing"
      overridePolicy: canApproveLargeAmounts
    }

    mutate status = "processed"
    emit RecordProcessed
  }
}

policy canApproveLargeAmounts override:
  user.role == "manager" or user.role == "admin"
  "Only managers and admins can approve large amounts"
```

If the override policy passes, the constraint outcome is marked `overridden: true` and the runtime emits a synthetic `OverrideApplied` event. Attempts to override a constraint that is not marked `overrideable` are always rejected.


---

## Events

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/events


Events in Manifest are first-class declarations. You define a schema, assign a channel, and commands reference the event by name using the `emit` action. Every emission is recorded in the runtime's event log with a payload, timestamp, and workflow metadata.

## Declaring an event

Use the `event` keyword followed by a name, a channel string, and an optional payload schema block:

```manifest
event TaskCompleted: "task.completed" {
  taskId: string
  completedBy: string
}

event CounterIncremented: "counter.incremented" {
  counterId: string
}
```

The channel (`"task.completed"`) is a free-form string. It appears on every `EmittedEvent` and can be used by subscribers to route messages.

Payload fields are typed with `string`, `number`, `boolean`, or `timestamp`. They serve as the schema contract — the runtime populates the payload from command input and the last action result.

## Emitting events from commands

Use the `emit` action inside a command body to emit an event:

```manifest
entity Counter {
  property value: number = 0

  command increment() {
    mutate value = value + 1
    emit CounterIncremented
  }

  command reset() {
    mutate value = 0
    emit CounterReset
  }
}

event CounterIncremented: "counter.incremented" {
  counterId: string
}

event CounterReset: "counter.reset" {
  counterId: string
}
```

A command can emit multiple events. They are emitted in declaration order after all actions complete successfully.

## EmittedEvent shape

Every emitted event is recorded as an `EmittedEvent` object in `CommandResult.emittedEvents`:

```typescript
interface EmittedEvent {
  name: string;        // Event name, e.g. "TaskCompleted"
  channel: string;     // Channel string, e.g. "task.completed"
  payload: object;     // Command input + last action result
  timestamp: number;   // Milliseconds since epoch (from runtime time source)
  emitIndex: number;   // Zero-based index within this runCommand invocation (vNext)

  // Workflow metadata (vNext — present when supplied in command options)
  correlationId?: string;
  causationId?: string;
}
```

The `payload` object contains the command's input parameters and the last action result. This gives event consumers enough context to reconstruct what triggered the emission.

## Workflow metadata (vNext)

Pass `correlationId` and `causationId` in the command options to propagate identifiers across a multi-step workflow. The runtime attaches them to every event emitted during that invocation.

```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  "Order",
  "complete",
  { instanceId: "order-123" },
  {
    correlationId: "workflow-abc",
    causationId: "step-2",
  }
);

// result.emittedEvents[0].correlationId === "workflow-abc"
// result.emittedEvents[0].causationId   === "step-2"
```

`emitIndex` is a per-command zero-based counter. It resets to `0` at the start of each `runCommand` call. It is not a global sequence — cross-command ordering is the caller's responsibility.


Given identical IR, identical runtime context, identical input, and identical options, emitted events will always have identical `emitIndex` values. This is a determinism guarantee.


## Subscribing to events

Use `runtime.onEvent(listener)` to register a listener. The listener receives **every** emitted event — filter by `event.name` inside the callback. It returns an unsubscribe function.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from "@angriff36/manifest";

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: "worker-1", role: "system" },
});

const unsubscribe = runtime.onEvent((event) => {
  if (event.name === "TaskCompleted") {
    console.log("Task completed:", event.payload);
    sendNotification(event.payload.completedBy);
  }
  if (event.name === "OrderCreated") {
    console.log("New order on channel:", event.channel);
    console.log("Correlation:", event.correlationId);
  }
});

// Call unsubscribe() when the listener is no longer needed.
```

Listeners fire synchronously during `runCommand`, after all actions and before `CommandResult` is returned.

## Complete example

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "pending"
  property total: number = 0

  command addItem(amount: number) {
    mutate total = self.total + amount
    emit OrderUpdated
  }

  command complete() {
    guard self.status == "in_progress"
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit OrderCompleted
  }
}

store Order in memory

event OrderUpdated: "order.updated" {
  orderId: string
  total: number
}

event OrderCompleted: "order.completed" {
  orderId: string
  finalTotal: number
}
```

```typescript
const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir);

runtime.onEvent((event) => {
  if (event.name === "OrderCompleted") {
    console.log(`Order ${event.payload.orderId} completed.`);
  }
});

const result = await runtime.runCommand("Order", "complete", {
  instanceId: "order-001",
});

console.log(result.emittedEvents);
// [
//   {
//     name: "OrderCompleted",
//     channel: "order.completed",
//     payload: { instanceId: "order-001", ... },
//     timestamp: 1748131200000,
//     emitIndex: 0
//   }
// ]
```

## OverrideApplied event (vNext)

When an authorized constraint override is applied, the runtime synthesizes and emits an `OverrideApplied` event. This event is not declared in your `.manifest` source — it is injected by the runtime automatically:

```typescript
{
  name: "OverrideApplied",
  channel: "system",
  payload: {
    constraintCode: "requiresApproval",
    reason: "Emergency processing authorized by finance lead",
    authorizedBy: "user-999",
    timestamp: 1748131200000,
    commandName: "process",
    entityName: "FinancialRecord",
    instanceId: "rec-42"
  }
}
```

This event appears in `CommandResult.emittedEvents` alongside any command-declared events.


---

## Stores

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/stores


A store declaration tells the runtime where to persist instances of a given entity. Every entity that participates in runtime command execution needs a corresponding store. The store target determines which adapter the runtime uses to read and write instances.

## Syntax

```manifest
store EntityName in <target>
```

`EntityName` must match an entity declared elsewhere in the same `.manifest` file (or module). The `<target>` is one of the built-in keywords or a custom identifier resolved at runtime configuration time.

## Built-in targets

### `memory`

Stores instances in a plain JavaScript `Map` keyed by instance ID. Data does not survive process restarts. This is the only target that is required to be supported by all conforming runtimes.

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required name: string
  property price: number = 0
}

store Product in memory
```

Use `memory` for:
- Local development and rapid iteration
- Unit and conformance tests
- Demos and prototyping

### `durable`

The **backend-neutral semantic target**. Declares that the entity is persisted in *some* durable store; the specific technology (Prisma, raw SQL, supabase-js, a custom adapter, …) is chosen by the consumer at runtime via a `storeProvider`, and at compile time via projection config.

```manifest
store Order in durable
```

Use `durable` when you want the entity to participate in storage projections without coupling the `.manifest` source to a specific backend. The [Prisma projection](/integration/prisma) treats `durable`, `postgres`, and `supabase` identically — all three are schema-emission targets. The runtime engine rejects `durable` at command-execution time unless a custom `storeProvider` is bound, with a clear "no store adapter supplied" diagnostic.


`durable` is the recommended target for new code. The legacy `postgres` and `supabase` targets continue to work; nothing was removed. Storage projections emit for all three.


### `postgres`

Delegates to a `PostgresStore` adapter. The adapter handles SQL reads and writes against a Postgres-compatible database. You must configure the adapter when constructing the `RuntimeEngine`.

```manifest
store Order in postgres
```


`postgres` is a target identifier, not a built-in adapter included in the core runtime. You must supply a Postgres adapter via `RuntimeEngine` options. See [Custom Stores](/adapters/custom-stores) for the adapter contract.


### `supabase`

Delegates to a `SupabaseStore` adapter. Similar to `postgres`, but typed for the Supabase client. Requires adapter configuration at runtime.

```manifest
store User in supabase
```


`supabase` is also an adapter target, not a built-in. Provide a Supabase-backed store via `RuntimeEngine` options.


## Multiple entities, multiple stores

Each entity gets its own `store` declaration. Entities in the same program can use different targets:

```manifest
entity Author {
  property required name: string
  hasMany books: Book
}

entity Book {
  property required title: string
  belongsTo author: Author
}

store Author in memory
store Book in memory
```

```manifest
entity User {
  property required email: string
  property required role: string = "member"
}

entity AuditLog {
  property required action: string
  property required actorId: string
  property createdAt: number = 0
}

store User in postgres
store AuditLog in postgres
```

## Using a custom store adapter

When you declare `store EntityName in postgres` (or any non-`memory` target), the `RuntimeEngine` must receive a `storeProvider` function that returns the appropriate adapter for that entity name:

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from "@angriff36/manifest";

class PostgresOrderStore implements Store<Order> {
  async getAll() {
    return await db.order.findMany();
  }

  async getById(id: string) {
    return await db.order.findUnique({ where: { id } }) ?? undefined;
  }

  async create(data: Partial<Order>) {
    return await db.order.create({ data });
  }

  async update(id: string, data: Partial<Order>) {
    return await db.order.update({ where: { id }, data });
  }

  async delete(id: string) {
    await db.order.delete({ where: { id } });
    return true;
  }

  async clear() {
    await db.order.deleteMany({});
  }
}

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: "user-1", role: "admin" },
  storeProvider: (entityName) => {
    if (entityName === "Order") return new PostgresOrderStore();
    return undefined; // fall back to memory store
  },
});
```

When `storeProvider` returns `undefined` for an entity, the runtime falls back to an in-memory store for that entity.


See [Custom Stores](/adapters/custom-stores) for the full `Store` interface contract and a guide to implementing production-ready adapters.


## Store and `localStorage`

The `localStorage` target is available for browser environments. Like `postgres` and `supabase`, it requires an adapter. The runtime does not bundle a `localStorage` implementation — you supply one via `storeProvider`.

```manifest
store Preferences in localStorage
```

## What the runtime does when a store is missing

If an entity is referenced in a command but has no store declaration, the runtime uses an in-memory store by default. The compiler may emit a warning for undeclared stores depending on your toolchain configuration.


Entities without an explicit store declaration have no persistence guarantee. In production, always declare an explicit store target to make your persistence intent clear and to enable adapter injection.


---

## Computed Properties

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/computed-properties


A computed property is a read-only value derived from an expression rather than stored directly. You declare it with the `computed` keyword inside an entity block. The runtime evaluates it on demand, using the current instance fields and any declared dependencies.

## Syntax

```manifest
computed <name>: <type> = <expression>
```

The expression can reference `self`, `this`, any property on the instance, other computed properties, and the standard built-ins (`now()`, `uuid()`).

```manifest
entity Item {
  property required price: number
  property quantity: number = 1
  property discountPercent: number = 0

  computed total: number = price * quantity
  computed discountedTotal: number = total * (1 - discountPercent / 100)
  computed hasDiscount: boolean = discountPercent > 0
  computed isExpensive: boolean = total > 100
}
```

## Evaluation context

When the runtime evaluates a computed property, the context contains:

| Binding | Value |
|---------|-------|
| `self` / `this` | The current entity instance |
| All property values | Direct field access by name |
| Any dependencies listed in IR | Other computed properties that have already resolved |
| `user` | The current user object (if the runtime provides one) |
| `context` | The runtime context object |

```manifest
entity Author {
  property required name: string
  hasMany books: Book

  computed bookCount: number = self.books.length
}
```

Relationship traversal (`self.books`) is fully supported in computed expressions. The runtime resolves the relationship synchronously before evaluating the expression.

## Chaining computed properties

A computed property can reference another computed property on the same entity. The runtime resolves them in dependency order:

```manifest
entity WorkflowStep {
  property status: string = "pending"
  property executedAt: number = 0

  computed isPending: boolean = self.status == "pending"
  computed isInProgress: boolean = self.status == "in_progress"
  computed isCompleted: boolean = self.status == "completed"
  computed canStart: boolean = self.isPending
  computed canComplete: boolean = self.isInProgress
}
```

`canStart` depends on `isPending`, which depends on `status`. The runtime evaluates in bottom-up order.

## When computed properties re-evaluate

Computed properties are evaluated fresh each time they are accessed — there is no caching layer in the default runtime. They reflect the state of the instance at the moment of access. This means:

- After a `mutate` action inside a command, any subsequent access to a computed property reflects the updated field values.
- Computed properties based on `now()` return a different value on each evaluation if the time source advances.

## Infinite recursion prevention

The runtime detects dependency cycles. If evaluating a computed property would cause infinite recursion, the runtime returns `undefined` for that property instead of throwing. This is defined behavior, not an error condition.

```manifest
entity Circular {
  // If A depends on B and B depends on A, both evaluate to undefined
  computed a: number = self.b + 1
  computed b: number = self.a + 1
}
```


A computed property that returns `undefined` due to a cycle will silently produce `undefined` in guard and action expressions. If you rely on a computed value being non-null, ensure there are no cycles in the dependency graph.


## Using computed properties in guards and actions

Computed properties are first-class values in the evaluation context. You can reference them in guards, policies, and action expressions:

```manifest
entity Task {
  property required title: string
  property required status: string = "todo"
  property required assigneeId: string = ""
  property required createdAt: number = 0

  computed isOverdue: boolean = (now() - self.createdAt) > 86400000 and self.status != "done"
  computed assignedUser: string = self.assigneeId != "" ? self.assigneeId : "Unassigned"
  computed isHighPriority: boolean = self.priority != null and self.priority >= 3

  command updateStatus(newStatus: string) {
    guard newStatus != null and newStatus != ""
    guard newStatus == "todo" or newStatus == "in-progress" or newStatus == "done"
    mutate status = newStatus
    emit TaskStatusUpdated
  }

  command assignTask(userId: string) {
    guard userId != null and userId != ""
    guard self.assigneeId == "" or user.role == "admin"
    mutate assigneeId = userId
    emit TaskAssigned
  }
}
```

Guards like `self.assigneeId == ""` reference the raw property. A guard referencing `self.isHighPriority` would evaluate the computed property first and use the result.

## Computed properties in the TypeScript output

When you use the Next.js projection or TypeScript types surface, computed properties appear as read-only fields on the generated interface. They are not persisted in the store — they are derived each time the instance is loaded.

```typescript
interface Task {
  title: string;
  status: string;
  assigneeId: string;
  createdAt: number;
  // Computed — derived at read time
  readonly isOverdue: boolean;
  readonly assignedUser: string;
  readonly isHighPriority: boolean;
}
```


---

## Integration Overview

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/integration/overview


Manifest gives you two distinct integration patterns: **Projections**, which generate platform-specific code from your compiled IR, and the **Embedded Runtime**, which lets you drive command execution directly inside your own handlers. Most production applications use both.

This page explains the boundary between them, how to decide which to reach for first, and a security rule that applies to both.

## The two patterns

<Tabs>
  <Tab title="Projections">
    Projections are a **code-generation tool**, not a change to language semantics. They read your compiled Intermediate Representation (IR) and emit artifacts—Next.js App Router routes, TypeScript types, and a client SDK—that you can drop straight into your project.

    ```bash
    # Compile + generate all surfaces for your .manifest file
    manifest build recipe.manifest --projection nextjs --code-output app/api/

    # Or use the CLI to generate individual surfaces from compiled IR
    manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface route --output app/api/
    manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface command --output app/api/
    ```

    The four generated surfaces are:

    | Surface | What it produces |
    | --- | --- |
    | `nextjs.route` | GET handlers with direct database queries |
    | `nextjs.command` | POST/PUT/DELETE handlers that call `RuntimeEngine.runCommand` |
    | `ts.types` | TypeScript interfaces derived from your entity definitions |
    | `ts.client` | Typed fetch wrappers for calling your routes from client code |

    **Read operations** in generated routes use direct database queries and intentionally bypass the runtime for performance. **Write operations** always flow through `RuntimeEngine.runCommand` so that guards, policies, constraints, and event emissions run in the order the spec requires.
  </Tab>
  <Tab title="Embedded Runtime">
    The Embedded Runtime lets you instantiate `RuntimeEngine` directly in your own handlers and call `runCommand` yourself. You control request parsing, response shaping, event routing, and any custom side-effect pipeline.

    ```typescript
    import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';

    const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
      user: { id: 'user-1', role: 'admin' },
      context: { requestId: 'req-1' },
    });

    const result = await runtime.runCommand(
      'approve',
      { reason: 'validated' },
      { entityName: 'Invoice', instanceId: 'inv-1' }
    );

    if (!result.success) {
      // result.error, result.guardFailure, result.policyDenial
      // Surface these in your response — do not alter execution semantics.
    }
    ```

    The runtime enforces the full execution order on every `runCommand` call:

    1. Build evaluation context (`self`, `this`, params, runtime context)
    2. Evaluate applicable policies
    3. Evaluate guards in declared order — halts on the first failure
    4. Execute actions in declared order
    5. Emit declared events
    6. Return `CommandResult`
  </Tab>
</Tabs>

## Decision guide

Use **Projections** when:

- You want generated route code that follows Next.js App Router conventions without writing boilerplate by hand.
- Your mutation flow can be expressed as a named Manifest command and you are comfortable with the generated handler structure.

Use the **Embedded Runtime** when:

- You need custom orchestration around command execution — for example, composing multiple commands, injecting computed context, or conditionally routing results.
- You need a custom event-handling pipeline: publishing to a queue, writing to an outbox, or triggering side effects in a specific order.


  These patterns are not mutually exclusive. A common setup uses generated routes for straightforward CRUD entities and the Embedded Runtime for commands that require complex orchestration.


## Security: never trust client-supplied identity fields

This rule applies regardless of which pattern you use.

When a command is executed from an HTTP handler, the request body is **untrusted**. A client can supply any value for `id`, `userId`, `tenantId`, `orgId`, or `user`. If those values flow unmodified into `runCommand`, a caller can act on behalf of another user or mutate a resource they do not own.

**Always strip client-supplied identity fields and inject authoritative values from your auth context and URL path parameters before calling `runCommand`.**

```typescript
// Correct: derive identity from trusted server-side sources
const { userId } = await auth();                  // from your auth provider
const { id: entityId } = params;                  // from the URL path param

const body = await request.json();

// Strip any identity fields the client may have sent
const { id: _id, userId: _userId, tenantId: _tenantId, orgId: _orgId, user: _user, ...safeInput } = body;

// Inject authoritative identity before execution
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'update',
  { ...safeInput, userId, entityId },
  { entityName: 'Invoice', instanceId: entityId }
);
```


  Do not pass `await request.json()` directly to `runCommand` without stripping identity fields. This is the most common source of privilege-escalation bugs in Manifest-backed APIs.


## Next steps

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="Projections" icon="wand-sparkles" href="/integration/projections">
    Learn how to use the projection API programmatically and understand the four generated surfaces.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Embedded Runtime" icon="cpu" href="/integration/embedded-runtime">
    Instantiate RuntimeEngine, handle CommandResult variants, and wire up event handlers.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Next.js guide" icon="triangle" href="/integration/nextjs">
    End-to-end walkthrough: install, write a `.manifest` file, generate routes, and add auth.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>


---

## Projections

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/integration/projections


A **projection** reads your compiled Intermediate Representation (IR) and emits platform-specific artifacts. Projections are tooling — they do not redefine runtime semantics. Adding or changing a projection never changes what a Manifest program *means*; it only changes what code gets generated from it.

## Available projections

| Projection | CLI identifier | What it generates | Guide |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Next.js | `nextjs` | App Router routes, dispatcher, TS types, client SDK | [Next.js projection](/integration/nextjs) |
| Prisma  | `prisma` | A `schema.prisma` file (models, relations, indexes) | [Prisma projection](/integration/prisma) |

Projections share a single contract — `ProjectionTarget.generate(ir, request)` returns one or more `ProjectionArtifact`s plus structured diagnostics. The CLI's `--projection` flag selects which projection runs; dispatch is generic (the registry resolves the name), so adding a new projection means registering it, not editing a hardcoded dispatch branch.

## What projections produce

The Next.js projection ships four surfaces:

| Surface | CLI identifier | What it generates |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Entity read routes | `nextjs.route` | Next.js App Router GET handlers using direct database queries |
| Command routes | `nextjs.command` | POST/PUT/DELETE handlers that call `RuntimeEngine.runCommand` |
| TypeScript types | `ts.types` | `interface` declarations derived from your IR entity definitions |
| Client SDK | `ts.client` | Typed fetch wrappers for calling your routes from browser or server-client code |

The Prisma projection ships a single surface:

| Surface | CLI identifier | What it generates |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Schema | `prisma.schema` | One `schema.prisma` file with models, `@relation` wiring, `@db.Decimal` precision, and projection-config-driven `@map`/`@@map`/`@@index` |

## Reads vs. writes

This is the most important rule for projections:

- **Read routes (`nextjs.route`) may bypass the runtime.** Generated GET handlers use direct database queries (Prisma by default). This is intentional — read policies are not enforced by default in Manifest semantics, and direct queries are more efficient.
- **Write routes (`nextjs.command`) MUST use `RuntimeEngine.runCommand`.** Bypassing `runCommand` for mutations bypasses guard evaluation, policy checks, constraint validation, and event emission. The spec requires these to run in a fixed order on every mutation.


  Never replace `RuntimeEngine.runCommand` with direct database writes in a command route. Doing so silently strips all semantic enforcement from that operation.


## Using the projection CLI

The CLI is a development tool that wraps the projection API. Use it during scaffolding to generate initial route files.

```bash
# Compile + generate all surfaces in one step
manifest build recipe.manifest --projection nextjs --code-output app/api/

# Generate a specific surface from compiled IR
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface route --output app/api/
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface command --output app/api/
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface types --output lib/manifest-types.ts
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface client --output lib/manifest-client.ts

# Configure the auth provider
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --auth clerk --output app/api/
```


  The CLI uses `tsx` and is intended for development-time scaffolding. For production build pipelines, call the projection functions directly in your build scripts.


## Using the projection API programmatically

Import `NextJsProjection` and call the appropriate generator method in your build script.

```typescript
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
import { NextJsProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections/nextjs';
import fs from 'fs/promises';

const source = await fs.readFile('recipe.manifest', 'utf-8');
const { ir, diagnostics } = await compileToIR(source);

if (diagnostics.some(d => d.severity === 'error')) {
  for (const d of diagnostics) console.error(d.message);
  process.exit(1);
}

const projection = new NextJsProjection();

// Generate the entity read route
const routeResult = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe');
await fs.writeFile('app/api/recipes/route.ts', routeResult.code);

// Generate TypeScript types
const typesResult = projection.generateTypes(ir);
await fs.writeFile('lib/manifest-types.ts', typesResult.code);

// Generate the client SDK
const clientResult = projection.generateClient(ir);
await fs.writeFile('lib/manifest-client.ts', clientResult.code);
```

### Configuration options

Pass an options object as the third argument to `generateRoute` to customize the output:

```typescript
const result = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', {
  authProvider: 'clerk',          // 'clerk' | 'nextauth' | 'custom' | 'none'
  authImportPath: '@/lib/auth',   // custom import path for auth utilities
  databaseImportPath: '@/lib/database',
  responseImportPath: '@/lib/manifest-response',
  includeTenantFilter: true,      // include `where: { tenantId }` in queries
  includeSoftDeleteFilter: true,  // include `where: { deletedAt: null }`
  tenantIdProperty: 'tenantId',   // property name for tenant isolation
  deletedAtProperty: 'deletedAt', // property name for soft-delete timestamps
  appDir: 'app/api',              // output directory prefix
  strictMode: true,               // emit TypeScript strict-mode compatible code
});
```

## Auth provider examples



```typescript Clerk
const result = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', {
  authProvider: 'clerk',
  // Generated import: import { auth } from "@clerk/nextjs/server";
});
```

```typescript NextAuth
const result = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', {
  authProvider: 'nextauth',
  // Generated import: import { getServerSession } from "next-auth";
});
```

```typescript Custom
const result = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', {
  authProvider: 'custom',
  authImportPath: '@/lib/my-auth',
  // Generated import: import { getUser } from "@/lib/my-auth";
});
```

```typescript None
const result = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', {
  authProvider: 'none',
  // Generated: const userId = "anonymous";
});
```



## Tenant and soft-delete filtering

```typescript
// Multi-tenant with soft delete (defaults)
const result = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', {
  includeTenantFilter: true,
  tenantIdProperty: 'tenantId',
  includeSoftDeleteFilter: true,
  deletedAtProperty: 'deletedAt',
  // Generated WHERE: { tenantId, deletedAt: null }
});

// Single-tenant, no soft delete
const result = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', {
  includeTenantFilter: false,
  includeSoftDeleteFilter: false,
  // Generated WHERE: { }
});

// Custom property names
const result = projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', {
  tenantIdProperty: 'orgId',
  deletedAtProperty: 'removedAt',
  // Generated WHERE: { orgId, removedAt: null }
});
```

## The projection boundary

Projections handle transport concerns: authentication, HTTP conventions, response shaping, and read-path strategy. They do not own runtime responsibilities.

| Projection owns | Runtime owns |
| --- | --- |
| Reading IR and emitting code | Building the evaluation context |
| Configuring auth and database imports | Enforcing policies |
| Choosing the read-path strategy | Evaluating guards in order |
| Adapting to framework conventions | Executing actions |
| | Emitting declared events |

Projections are tooling. They cannot introduce new language semantics — only the runtime and its specification can define how Manifest programs behave. If you find a mismatch between what a generated route does and what the Manifest spec requires, the generated code is wrong, not the spec.


---

## Embedded Runtime

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/integration/embedded-runtime


The Embedded Runtime pattern gives you direct access to `RuntimeEngine` in your own handlers. Use it when you need custom orchestration around command execution, a bespoke event-handling pipeline, or precise control over how `CommandResult` maps to your API response format.

## When to use this pattern

Reach for the Embedded Runtime when:

- You need to compose multiple commands in a single request.
- You want to publish emitted events to a queue, outbox, or tracing system using your own logic.
- You need custom response shaping that does not match generated route conventions.
- You are writing a framework adapter for a platform that does not have a Manifest projection yet.

For straightforward CRUD routes in Next.js, consider the [Projections pattern](/integration/projections) instead — it generates the boilerplate for you.

## Minimal flow

1. Compile your `.manifest` source to IR, or load a pre-built IR artifact.
2. Construct `RuntimeEngine` with the IR and a runtime context object.
3. Call `runCommand` for each mutation.
4. Inspect `CommandResult` and handle emitted events in your application code.

## Instantiating RuntimeEngine

```typescript
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';

const source = await fs.readFile('invoice.manifest', 'utf-8');
const { ir, diagnostics } = await compileToIR(source);

if (!ir) {
  throw new Error('Compilation failed');
}

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'user-1', role: 'admin' },
  context: { requestId: 'req-abc' },
});
```

The second argument is the **runtime context**. If your guards or policies reference `user` or `context` bindings, you must supply those fields here. Omitting required context is a valid execution failure — the runtime will not inject permissive defaults.

## Calling runCommand

```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'approve',
  { reason: 'validated' },
  { entityName: 'Invoice', instanceId: 'inv-1' }
);
```

`runCommand` takes three arguments:

1. **Command name** — the name of the command as declared in your `.manifest` file.
2. **Input** — the command's input parameters as a plain object.
3. **Options** — an object with `entityName` and `instanceId`.

The runtime executes in this fixed order on every call:

1. Build evaluation context (`self`, `this`, params, runtime context)
2. Evaluate applicable policies — denies halt execution immediately
3. Evaluate guards in declared order — the first falsey guard halts execution
4. Evaluate command constraints — `block`-severity violations halt execution
5. Execute actions in declared order (`mutate`, `emit`, `compute`)
6. Emit declared events
7. Return `CommandResult`

## Handling CommandResult

`CommandResult` has a `success` boolean. When `success` is `false`, inspect the failure reason:

```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'approve',
  { reason: 'validated' },
  { entityName: 'Invoice', instanceId: 'inv-1' }
);

if (result.success) {
  // Command executed; access emitted events
  console.log('Emitted events:', result.events);
} else {
  // Surface diagnostics — do not alter or suppress them.
  if (result.guardFailure) {
    // Guard failed: result.guardFailure contains the failing guard index and expression
    console.error('Guard failed:', result.guardFailure);
  }

  if (result.policyDenial) {
    // A policy denied execution
    console.error('Policy denied:', result.policyDenial);
  }

  console.error('Error:', result.error);
}
```


  Diagnostics explain failures; they never compensate for them. Do not catch `CommandResult` failures and silently succeed — surface the failure to the caller with an appropriate HTTP status code.


## Handling events with onEvent

Use `onEvent` to register a listener before calling `runCommand`. The listener receives events in emission order as the command executes.

```typescript
const unsubscribe = runtime.onEvent((event) => {
  console.log(event.name, event.channel, event.payload);

  // Publish to a queue, write to an outbox, send a webhook, etc.
});

try {
  await runtime.runCommand(
    'approve',
    {},
    { entityName: 'Invoice', instanceId: 'inv-1' }
  );
} finally {
  unsubscribe();
}
```

`onEvent` returns an unsubscribe function. Call it in a `finally` block to avoid listener leaks across requests.

## Request identity hardening

When `runCommand` is called from an HTTP handler, **strip all client-supplied identity fields from the request body** before passing input to the runtime. A client can supply arbitrary values for `id`, `userId`, `tenantId`, `orgId`, or `user`. Passing those values directly enables parameter tampering and identity spoofing.

Derive identity exclusively from:
- Your auth provider (e.g., Clerk's `auth()`, NextAuth's `getServerSession`)
- URL path parameters
